title: Check if graph is bipartite (DFS coloring).
description: Check if graph is bipartite (DFS coloring).

examples.explanation: Step 1: V=4. Build adjacency matrix: row0: [F,T,F,T]; row1: [T,F,T,F]; row2: [F,T,F,T]; row3: [T,F,T,F]. Step 2: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. DFS from vertex 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors: 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; 3 (uncolored) -> colors[3]=1, push 3. Stack=[1,3]. Pop 3: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=0, push 2. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2: neighbors: 1 (colored 1 != 0, OK); 3 (colored 1 != 0, OK). Pop 1: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (colored 0 != 1, OK). DFS done. Step 3: Pair check: pairs (0,1): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK; (0,2): not adjacent; (0,3): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK; (1,2): adjacent, colors 1!=0 OK; (1,3): not adjacent; (2,3): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK. All OK, return True.

Step 1: V=4. Build matrix: row0: [F,T,T,T]; row1: [T,F,T,F]; row2: [T,T,F,T]; row3: [T,F,T,F]. Step 2: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. DFS from vertex 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors: 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=1, push 2; 3 (uncolored) -> colors[3]=1, push 3. Stack=[1,2,3]. Pop 3: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (colored 1 == 1, conflict!). Return False immediately. No pair check needed.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V^2)
approaches.summary: Check all pairs of vertices to ensure no two adjacent vertices share the same color, using a naive adjacency list or matrix. This method is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Bipartite Graph Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if a graph is bipartite by examining all vertex pairs for adjacency conflicts.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the bipartite graph problem by systematically checking every possible pair of vertices to ensure no two adjacent vertices share the same color. The intuition is that a graph is bipartite if we can color it with two colors such that no edge connects vertices of the same color. We represent the graph using an adjacency matrix for O(1) edge lookup. We iterate through all vertices, assigning colors (0 or 1) via DFS. For each vertex, we check all other vertices: if they're adjacent and have the same color, the graph isn't bipartite. The algorithm starts with an uncolored array, uses a stack for DFS, and for each vertex visited, colors it and pushes its neighbors. For each neighbor, if uncolored, assign opposite color; if colored, check if it matches current vertex's color—if yes, return false. After DFS, we verify all pairs: for each vertex i, for each vertex j > i, if adjacency_matrix[i][j] is true and colors[i] == colors[j], return false. This double-check ensures no missed conflicts. Time complexity is O(V²) due to nested loops over vertices for pair checking and adjacency matrix construction. Space complexity is O(V²) for the matrix. Connection to L1: This uses the adjacency conflict checker as a final verification step. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (DFS must start from each unvisited vertex) and incorrect color initialization. Real code pattern: def is_bipartite_bruteforce(adj_list): V = len(adj_list); matrix = [[False]*V for _ in range(V)]; for i in range(V): for j in adj_list[i]: matrix[i][j] = True; colors = [-1]*V; for start in range(V): if colors[start] == -1: stack = [start]; colors[start] = 0; while stack: v = stack.pop(); for u in range(V): if matrix[v][u]: if colors[u] == -1: colors[u] = 1 - colors[v]; stack.append(u); elif colors[u] == colors[v]: return False; for i in range(V): for j in range(i+1, V): if matrix[i][j] and colors[i] == colors[j]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: V=4. Build adjacency matrix: row0: [F,T,F,T]; row1: [T,F,T,F]; row2: [F,T,F,T]; row3: [T,F,T,F]. Step 2: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. DFS from vertex 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors: 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; 3 (uncolored) -> colors[3]=1, push 3. Stack=[1,3]. Pop 3: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=0, push 2. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2: neighbors: 1 (colored 1 != 0, OK); 3 (colored 1 != 0, OK). Pop 1: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (colored 0 != 1, OK). DFS done. Step 3: Pair check: pairs (0,1): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK; (0,2): not adjacent; (0,3): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK; (1,2): adjacent, colors 1!=0 OK; (1,3): not adjacent; (2,3): adjacent, colors 0!=1 OK. All OK, return True.

Step 1: V=4. Build matrix: row0: [F,T,T,T]; row1: [T,F,T,F]; row2: [T,T,F,T]; row3: [T,F,T,F]. Step 2: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. DFS from vertex 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors: 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=1, push 2; 3 (uncolored) -> colors[3]=1, push 3. Stack=[1,2,3]. Pop 3: neighbors: 0 (colored 0 != 1, OK); 2 (colored 1 == 1, conflict!). Return False immediately. No pair check needed.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Adjacency Conflict Checker
ladders.desc: Verify no two adjacent vertices share the same color by checking all vertex pairs.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step in the brute force approach. After coloring vertices via DFS, we must ensure no edge connects vertices of the same color. The intuition is that even if DFS doesn't detect conflicts during traversal (e.g., in complex cycles), a final exhaustive check guarantees correctness. We use an adjacency matrix for efficient edge lookup. The algorithm iterates through all pairs of vertices (i, j) where i < j to avoid duplicates. For each pair, if there's an edge between i and j (adjacency_matrix[i][j] is true) and colors[i] equals colors[j], we return false immediately. Otherwise, after checking all pairs, return true. Time complexity is O(V²) due to nested loops over V vertices. Space complexity is O(1) extra space, but relies on the O(V²) matrix. Connection to L0: This is the final step in the full solution, providing a safety net against missed conflicts. Connection to L2: It builds on the adjacency matrix representation. Common mistakes include not using i < j (checking pairs twice) or forgetting to verify the matrix is symmetric for undirected graphs. Real code pattern: def check_conflicts(matrix, colors): V = len(colors); for i in range(V): for j in range(i+1, V): if matrix[i][j] and colors[i] == colors[j]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: V=4. Pairs: (0,1): matrix[0][1]=T, colors 0!=1 OK; (0,2): matrix[0][2]=F, skip; (0,3): matrix[0][3]=T, colors 0!=1 OK; (1,2): matrix[1][2]=T, colors 1!=0 OK; (1,3): matrix[1][3]=F, skip; (2,3): matrix[2][3]=T, colors 0!=1 OK. All pairs OK, return True.

Step 1: V=4. Pairs: (0,1): matrix[0][1]=T, colors 0!=1 OK; (0,2): matrix[0][2]=T, colors 0!=1 OK; (0,3): matrix[0][3]=T, colors 0!=1 OK; (1,2): matrix[1][2]=T, colors 1==1 conflict! Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DFS Coloring with Conflict Detection
ladders.desc: Color graph vertices using DFS and detect immediate conflicts during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for attempting to color the graph with two colors. The intuition is that we can traverse the graph depth-first, assigning alternating colors (0 and 1) to adjacent vertices. If at any point we encounter an adjacent vertex already colored with the same color, we've found a conflict, indicating the graph may not be bipartite. The algorithm starts with an array colors initialized to -1 (uncolored). For each unvisited vertex, we start DFS using a stack: push the vertex, color it with 0. While the stack isn't empty, pop a vertex v, and for each neighbor u (using adjacency list), if u is uncolored, assign color 1 - colors[v] (i.e., the opposite color), and push u. If u is already colored, check if colors[u] equals colors[v]; if yes, return false immediately for a conflict. This handles connected components. Time complexity is O(V + E) for the DFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for colors and stack. Connection to L1: This provides the initial coloring, but may miss conflicts in certain graph structures, hence the need for the final pair check. Connection to L3: It relies on graph traversal concepts. Common mistakes include not handling multiple components (must iterate over all vertices) and incorrect color toggling (use 1-color, not color+1). Real code pattern: def dfs_coloring(adj_list): V = len(adj_list); colors = [-1]*V; for start in range(V): if colors[start] == -1: stack = [start]; colors[start] = 0; while stack: v = stack.pop(); for u in adj_list[v]: if colors[u] == -1: colors[u] = 1 - colors[v]; stack.append(u); elif colors[u] == colors[v]: return False, colors; return True, colors.

examples.trace: Step 1: V=4, colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; neighbor 3 (uncolored) -> colors[3]=1, push 3. Stack=[1,3]. Pop 3: neighbors 0 (colored 0 !=1 OK), 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=0, push 2. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2: neighbors 1 (colored 1 !=0 OK), 3 (colored 1 !=0 OK). Pop 1: neighbors 0 (colored 0 !=1 OK), 2 (colored 0 !=1 OK). All vertices visited, no conflicts, return True.

Step 1: V=3, colors=[-1,-1,-1]. Start at 0: stack=[0], colors[0]=0. Pop 0: neighbors 1 (uncolored) -> colors[1]=1, push 1; neighbor 2 (uncolored) -> colors[2]=1, push 2. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2: neighbors 0 (colored 0 !=1 OK), 1 (colored 1 ==1 conflict!). Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Convert an adjacency list to a matrix for O(1) edge lookup.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating an adjacency matrix from an adjacency list to enable efficient edge existence checks. The intuition is that while adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs, matrices provide constant-time access to check if an edge exists between any two vertices, which is crucial for the brute force pair checking. For an undirected graph with V vertices, we create a V x V boolean matrix initialized to False. We iterate through the adjacency list: for each vertex i, for each neighbor j in adj_list[i], set matrix[i][j] = True and matrix[j][i] = True (since the graph is undirected). This ensures symmetry. Time complexity is O(V + E) to process all edges, but since E can be up to O(V²) in dense graphs, it's O(V²) in worst case. Space complexity is O(V²) for the matrix. Connection to L2: The matrix is used in DFS for neighbor iteration (though list is typically used there) and essential for L1's conflict checker. Connection to L4: It builds on array indexing operations. Common mistakes include not handling undirected graphs (must set both matrix[i][j] and matrix[j][i]), or incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: def build_adj_matrix(adj_list): V = len(adj_list); matrix = [[False]*V for _ in range(V)]; for i in range(V): for j in adj_list[i]: matrix[i][j] = True; matrix[j][i] = True; return matrix.

examples.trace: Step 1: V=4, initialize 4x4 matrix all False. Step 2: Vertex 0: neighbors 1 and 3. Set matrix[0][1]=T, matrix[1][0]=T; matrix[0][3]=T, matrix[3][0]=T. Vertex 1: neighbors 0 and 2. Set matrix[1][0]=T (already T), matrix[0][1]=T (already T); matrix[1][2]=T, matrix[2][1]=T. Vertex 2: neighbors 1 and 3. Set matrix[2][1]=T (already T), matrix[1][2]=T (already T); matrix[2][3]=T, matrix[3][2]=T. Vertex 3: neighbors 0 and 2. Set matrix[3][0]=T (already T), matrix[0][3]=T (already T); matrix[3][2]=T (already T), matrix[2][3]=T (already T). Final matrix as above.

Step 1: V=2, initialize 2x2 matrix all False. Step 2: Vertex 0: neighbor 1. Set matrix[0][1]=T, matrix[1][0]=T. Vertex 1: neighbor 0. Set matrix[1][0]=T (already T), matrix[0][1]=T (already T). Final matrix as above.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Pairs of Vertices
ladders.desc: Loop through all unique pairs (i, j) where i < j in a set of vertices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves generating all combinations of two vertices from a set of V vertices, which is essential for the brute force conflict check. The intuition is that to check every possible edge for color conflicts, we need to examine each pair exactly once. We use nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to V-1, and the inner loop iterates j from i+1 to V-1. This ensures i < j, avoiding duplicate pairs like (1,0) after checking (0,1), and skipping self-pairs (i, i). For each pair, we perform an operation—in the full problem, checking adjacency and color equality. Time complexity is O(V²) as the number of pairs is V*(V-1)/2. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. Connection to L3: This operation is applied to the adjacency matrix rows/columns. Connection to L5: It builds on simple loop constructs. Common mistakes include using j starting from 0 (leads to duplicates and self-pairs) or incorrect range boundaries. Real code pattern: for i in range(V): for j in range(i+1, V): # process pair (i, j).

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0: j from 1 to 2: pair (0,1), then (0,2). Step 2: i=1: j from 2 to 2: pair (1,2). Step 3: i=2: j from 3 to 2 (no iteration). Total 3 pairs.
Step 1: i=0: j=1,2,3: pairs (0,1),(0,2),(0,3). Step 2: i=1: j=2,3: pairs (1,2),(1,3). Step 3: i=2: j=3: pair (2,3). Step 4: i=3: j from 4 to 3 (no iteration). Total 6 pairs.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Vertex Coloring with Two Colors
ladders.desc: Assign one of two colors to each vertex such that adjacent vertices have different colors.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what it means to color a graph with two colors. A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting them. Coloring means assigning a color to each vertex—here, we use two colors, often represented as 0 and 1. The key rule is that if two vertices are connected by an edge (adjacent), they must have different colors. This is the definition of a bipartite graph. The intuition is that we can split vertices into two sets (e.g., color 0 and color 1) where all edges go between the sets, not within them. For example, in a tree (which is always bipartite), we can alternate colors level by level. If we try to color a triangle (3 vertices all connected), it's impossible with two colors because you'd need three colors to avoid same-color edges. This concept is foundational because the entire problem reduces to checking if such a coloring exists. We use an array to store colors, with -1 meaning uncolored. Starting from any vertex, we color it 0, then its neighbors must be 1, their neighbors must be 0, and so on. If we ever need to color a vertex that's already colored with the wrong color, we have a conflict. Time and space aren't analyzed here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This coloring is applied when iterating over pairs. Common mistakes include misunderstanding adjacency or color assignment rules. Real code pattern: colors = [-1]*V; colors[start] = 0; for neighbor in adj_list[start]: colors[neighbor] = 1; etc.

examples.trace: Step 1: Color vertex 0 with 0. Step 2: Vertex 1 is adjacent to 0, so must be color 1. Step 3: Vertex 2 is adjacent to 1, so must be color 0 (different from 1). Check edges: 0-1: colors 0 and 1 different OK; 1-2: colors 1 and 0 different OK. Valid.

Step 1: Color vertex 0 with 0. Step 2: Vertex 1 adjacent to 0, so color 1. Step 3: Vertex 2 adjacent to 1, so must be color 0. But vertex 2 is also adjacent to 0, which is color 0—conflict! Cannot color with two colors.

approaches.name: DFS Coloring O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform depth-first search to assign alternating colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to vertices, checking for conflicts where adjacent vertices have the same color. This approach efficiently handles connected and disconnected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Graph is Bipartite Using DFS Coloring
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DFS to assign alternating colors and detect conflicts in both connected and disconnected graphs.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that a graph is bipartite if and only if it can be colored with two colors such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color. This property is equivalent to the graph having no odd-length cycles. DFS coloring works by traversing the graph depth-first, assigning alternating colors (0 and 1) to vertices. Starting from an uncolored vertex, we assign it a color (e.g., 0), then recursively visit all its neighbors. For each neighbor, if it's uncolored, we assign the opposite color and continue DFS; if it's already colored, we check if it conflicts with the current vertex's color. A conflict occurs when adjacent vertices have the same color, indicating the graph is not bipartite. We must handle disconnected graphs by checking all components. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time as each vertex and edge is processed once, and uses O(V) space for the color array and recursion stack.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an array 'color' of size V with -1 (uncolored).
2. For each vertex v from 0 to V-1:
   a. If color[v] == -1 (uncolored), start DFS from v with initial color 0.
   b. In DFS(u, current_color):
      i. Set color[u] = current_color.
      ii. For each neighbor w of u:
          - If color[w] == -1, recursively call DFS(w, 1-current_color).
          - Else if color[w] == current_color, return false (conflict).
      iii. Return true if no conflicts.
3. If all DFS calls return true, the graph is bipartite; otherwise, false.

Connection to L1: This level integrates the DFS coloring subroutine (L1) across all graph components.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs by iterating over all vertices, not resetting color array for each test case, and incorrect base cases in DFS.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def isBipartite(graph):
    V = len(graph)
    color = [-1]*V
    def dfs(u, c):
        color[u] = c
        for v in graph[u]:
            if color[v] == -1:
                if not dfs(v, 1-c):
                    return False
            elif color[v] == c:
                return False
        return True
    for i in range(V):
        if color[i] == -1:
            if not dfs(i, 0):
                return False
    return True

examples.trace: V=4, color=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0: color[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 3. For neighbor 1: color[1]==-1, dfs(1,1): color[1]=1, neighbors: 0 and 2. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 2: color[2]==-1, dfs(2,0): color[2]=0, neighbors: 1 and 3. Neighbor 1: color[1]=1 != 0, ok. Neighbor 3: color[3]==-1, dfs(3,1): color[3]=1, neighbors: 0 and 2. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 2: color[2]=0 != 1, ok. dfs(3) returns true. dfs(2) returns true. dfs(1) returns true. Back to vertex 0: neighbor 3 already colored 1 != 0, ok. dfs(0) returns true. All vertices colored, no conflicts, return true.

V=4, color=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0: color[0]=0. Neighbors: 1,2,3. For neighbor 1: color[1]==-1, dfs(1,1): color[1]=1, neighbors: 0 and 2. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 2: color[2]==-1, dfs(2,0): color[2]=0, neighbors: 0,1,3. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 == 0 (conflict with current color 0), return false. dfs(1) returns false. dfs(0) returns false. Graph not bipartite.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Coloring for a Connected Component
ladders.desc: DFS function that colors a connected component and checks for color conflicts.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the heart of the bipartite checking algorithm. The intuition is that within a connected component, we can determine bipartiteness by attempting a 2-coloring via DFS. Starting from a vertex, we assign it a color (0 or 1), then recursively visit its neighbors. For each neighbor, if uncolored, we assign the opposite color and continue; if already colored, we check if it matches the current vertex's color—a match indicates a conflict, meaning the component is not bipartite. This works because bipartite graphs are 2-colorable, and DFS ensures we explore all vertices in the component systematically.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define dfs(u, current_color):
   a. Set color[u] = current_color.
   b. For each neighbor v of u:
      i. If color[v] == -1 (uncolored):
         - Recursively call dfs(v, 1-current_color).
         - If it returns false, propagate false upward.
      ii. Else if color[v] == current_color:
         - Return false (conflict found).
   c. Return true (no conflicts in this branch).

Time complexity: O(V+E) for the component, as each vertex and edge is visited once. Space complexity: O(V) for recursion stack and color array.

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each uncolored vertex in the full problem to handle all components.

Common mistakes: Not passing the correct opposite color (1-current_color), forgetting to check for conflicts before recursing, and infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without proper base case.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def dfs(u, c):
    color[u] = c
    for v in graph[u]:
        if color[v] == -1:
            if not dfs(v, 1-c):
                return False
        elif color[v] == c:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace: color=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. dfs(0,0): color[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: color[1]==-1, dfs(1,1): color[1]=1, neighbors: 0 and 3. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 3: color[3]==-1, dfs(3,0): color[3]=0, neighbors: 1 and 2. Neighbor 1: color[1]=1 != 0, ok. Neighbor 2: color[2]==-1, dfs(2,1): color[2]=1, neighbors: 0 and 3. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 3: color[3]=0 != 1, ok. dfs(2) returns true. dfs(3) returns true. dfs(1) returns true. Back to vertex 0: neighbor 2 already colored 1 != 0, ok. dfs(0) returns true.

color=[-1,-1,-1]. dfs(0,0): color[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: color[1]==-1, dfs(1,1): color[1]=1, neighbors: 0 and 2. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Neighbor 2: color[2]==-1, dfs(2,0): color[2]=0, neighbors: 0 and 1. Neighbor 0: color[0]=0 == 0 (conflict), return false. dfs(1) returns false. dfs(0) returns false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color Assignment and Conflict Detection
ladders.desc: The central technique of assigning opposite colors to adjacent vertices and checking for same-color neighbors.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight for bipartite checking is that if a graph is bipartite, we can partition vertices into two sets such that all edges cross between the sets, with no edges within a set. This translates to a 2-coloring problem: assign colors 0 and 1 to vertices such that adjacent vertices have different colors. The logic involves two key operations: (1) When moving from a vertex u to an uncolored neighbor v, assign v the opposite color of u (i.e., 1 - color[u]). This ensures alternation along paths. (2) When encountering an already colored neighbor v, check if color[v] equals color[u]; if so, a conflict exists, indicating an odd cycle and non-bipartiteness. This works because in a bipartite graph, any path must alternate colors, so a conflict implies a path that returns to the same color, which can only happen with an odd number of edges (odd cycle).

Step-by-step logic for processing a vertex u with color c:
1. For each neighbor v of u:
   a. If v is uncolored: assign color[v] = 1-c and continue.
   b. If v is colored: check if color[v] == c; if true, report conflict.

Time complexity: O(degree(u)) per vertex. Space: O(1) for this logic alone.

Connection to L1: This logic is embedded within the DFS subroutine to handle each vertex's neighbors.

Common mistakes: Using incorrect opposite color (e.g., c+1 instead of 1-c), not handling the case where neighbor is colored but different (which is fine), and missing conflicts in undirected graphs by checking only one direction.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
for v in graph[u]:
    if color[v] == -1:
        color[v] = 1 - color[u]  # Assign opposite color
    elif color[v] == color[u]:
        return False  # Conflict detected

examples.input: Vertex u=0 with color=0, neighbors=[1,2], current colors: color=[0,-1,-1]
examples.trace: Process neighbor 1: color[1]==-1, assign color[1]=1-0=1. Process neighbor 2: color[2]==-1, assign color[2]=1-0=1. No conflicts, colors become [0,1,1].

examples.input: Vertex u=1 with color=1, neighbors=[0,2], current colors: color=[0,1,0]
examples.trace: Process neighbor 0: color[0]=0 != 1, ok. Process neighbor 2: color[2]=0 != 1, ok. Wait, no conflict here. Let's correct: Vertex u=1 with color=1, neighbors=[0,2], current colors: color=[1,1,0]. Process neighbor 0: color[0]=1 == 1, conflict detected, return false.

testCases.expected: No conflict, assign color[1]=1 (already 1 ok), color[3]=1 but color[3]=0 !=1, no conflict
No conflict, assign color[2]=0 (already 0 ok), color[4]=0 but color[4]=1 !=0, no conflict

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS
ladders.desc: Depth-first search to visit all vertices in a connected component.
ladders.explanation:
DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In the context of bipartite checking, DFS ensures we visit all vertices in a connected component systematically, allowing us to apply the 2-coloring logic consistently. The intuition is to start from a source vertex, mark it as visited (or colored), and recursively visit all its unvisited neighbors. This deep exploration helps in detecting cycles and ensuring all vertices are processed. For bipartite checking, we use DFS to propagate colors: when we move from a vertex u to a neighbor v, we assign v the opposite color, and DFS continues this process depth-first.

Step-by-step algorithm for DFS traversal:
1. Define dfs(u):
   a. Mark u as visited (e.g., set visited[u] = True or color[u] = some value).
   b. For each neighbor v of u:
      i. If v is not visited:
         - Recursively call dfs(v).

Time complexity: O(V+E) for the component. Space: O(V) for recursion stack and visited array.

Connection to L2: DFS provides the traversal framework within which the alternating color logic is applied.

Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs, infinite recursion in graphs with cycles without a visited check, and stack overflow for deep recursion (can be mitigated with iterative DFS).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def dfs(u):
    visited[u] = True
    for v in graph[u]:
        if not visited[v]:
            dfs(v)

examples.output: Visits vertices in order: 0,1,3,2 or similar
examples.trace: Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Pick neighbor 1 first: visited[1]=False, dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 3. Neighbor 0 visited, skip. Neighbor 3: visited[3]=False, dfs(3): mark visited[3]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Neighbor 1 visited, skip. Neighbor 2: visited[2]=False, dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 3, both visited, return. Back to dfs(3) returns. Back to dfs(1) returns. Back to dfs(0): process neighbor 2, already visited, skip. Traversal order: 0,1,3,2.

Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1. visited[1]=False, dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 2. Neighbor 0 visited, skip. Neighbor 2: visited[2]=False, dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 1, visited, return. Back to dfs(1) returns. Back to dfs(0) returns. Order: 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Neighbors in an Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Iterating through the neighbors of a vertex in a graph represented as an adjacency list.
ladders.explanation:
In graph algorithms, we often need to access all vertices adjacent to a given vertex. An adjacency list is a common representation where each vertex has a list of its neighbors. This operation is fundamental because it allows us to explore edges efficiently. For bipartite checking, we access neighbors to assign colors and detect conflicts. The intuition is simple: for a vertex u, graph[u] gives a list of vertices v such that there is an edge (u,v). We iterate through this list to process each neighbor. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows O(degree(u)) time to access neighbors.

Step-by-step operation:
1. Given a graph represented as a list of lists: graph = [list of neighbors for vertex 0, list for vertex 1, ...].
2. For a vertex u, the neighbors are graph[u].
3. Iterate through each neighbor v in graph[u]:
   a. Perform operations like checking color[v] or assigning colors.

Time complexity: O(degree(u)) per vertex. Space: O(1) for the iteration.

Connection to L3: This operation is used within DFS to traverse to neighboring vertices.

Common mistakes: Assuming graph is symmetric for undirected graphs (it should be), indexing errors (e.g., graph[u] for u out of range), and not handling empty neighbor lists.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
for v in graph[u]:
    # Process neighbor v

examples.trace: graph[0] returns the list [1,2]. Iterating: first neighbor v=1, then v=2.
graph[1] returns [0,2]. Iterating: first neighbor v=0, then v=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understanding how a graph is stored as a list of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex and its list contains neighbors.
ladders.explanation:
A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting them. To work with graphs in algorithms, we need a way to represent them in code. An adjacency list is a common and efficient representation, especially for sparse graphs. In this representation, we use a list (or array) where each element corresponds to a vertex. For vertex i, graph[i] is a list of all vertices j such that there is an edge from i to j (for directed graphs) or between i and j (for undirected graphs). For undirected graphs, if there's an edge between i and j, j appears in graph[i] and i appears in graph[j]. This representation is intuitive because it directly shows connections: for example, graph = [[1,2], [0,3], [0,3], [1,2]] means vertex 0 is connected to vertices 1 and 2, vertex 1 to 0 and 3, etc. It saves space compared to an adjacency matrix when the graph has few edges (O(V+E) vs O(V^2)). For beginners, think of it as a friendship list: each person has a list of their friends.

Step-by-step to create an adjacency list from edge list:
1. Initialize an empty list graph of size V (number of vertices).
2. For each edge (u,v):
   a. Append v to graph[u].
   b. If undirected, also append u to graph[v].

Time to access neighbors: O(degree(vertex)). Space: O(V+E).

Connection to L4: This representation enables the basic operation of accessing neighbors.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize lists for all vertices, not adding both directions for undirected graphs, and off-by-one errors in vertex numbering (usually 0-indexed).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
V = 4  # number of vertices
graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]
# Add edge (0,1)
graph[0].append(1)
graph[1].append(0)  # if undirected

examples.trace: Start with graph = [[],[],[],[]]. Add edge (0,1): append 1 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[1]. Graph: [[1],[0],[],[]]. Add edge (0,2): append 2 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[2]. Graph: [[1,2],[0],[0],[]]. Add edge (1,3): append 3 to graph[1], append 1 to graph[3]. Graph: [[1,2],[0,3],[0],[1]]. Add edge (2,3): append 3 to graph[2], append 2 to graph[3]. Final: [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]].
Start graph = [[],[],[]]. Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1), graph[1].append(0). Graph: [[1],[0],[]]. Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2), graph[2].append(1). Final: [[1],[0,2],[1]].

approaches.name: BFS Coloring O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search to color vertices level by level, ensuring no adjacent vertices share the same color by checking neighbors during traversal. This method is iterative and works well for avoiding recursion depth issues.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS Coloring for Bipartite Graph Detection
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using BFS to check if an undirected graph is bipartite by coloring vertices level by level.
ladders.explanation: The BFS coloring approach solves the bipartite graph detection problem by systematically assigning colors to vertices while traversing the graph breadth-first. A graph is bipartite if its vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects vertices from different sets, which translates to a 2-coloring problem where adjacent vertices must have different colors. The intuition is that BFS processes vertices level by level, allowing us to color each vertex opposite to its parent's color. If we ever encounter a neighbor that already has the same color as the current vertex, we've found an edge connecting two vertices of the same color, proving the graph is not bipartite. The algorithm starts by initializing a color array with -1 (uncolored) for all vertices. For each uncolored vertex (to handle disconnected graphs), we start a BFS: set its color to 0, push it to a queue, then process each vertex by checking all its neighbors. For each neighbor, if uncolored, assign the opposite color and enqueue it; if already colored with the same color as current vertex, return false immediately. If BFS completes for all vertices without conflicts, the graph is bipartite. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the color array and queue. This approach avoids recursion depth issues common in DFS and works efficiently for large graphs. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to check all vertices), not using proper BFS queue operations, and incorrect color assignment logic. The real code pattern uses a queue for BFS, a color array, and adjacency list representation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0 (uncolored). Set colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process vertex 0: neighbors [1,2,3]. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set colors[1]=1, queue=[1]. For neighbor 2: uncolored, set colors[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. For neighbor 3: uncolored, set colors[3]=1, queue=[1,2,3]. Step 3: Process vertex 1: color=1, neighbors [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: color=0 (different, OK). Check neighbor 2: color=1 (same as vertex 1's color). Conflict found: edge (1,2) connects same color vertices. Return false.

Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0 (uncolored). Set colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process vertex 0: neighbors [1,3]. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set colors[1]=1, queue=[1]. For neighbor 3: uncolored, set colors[3]=1, queue=[1,3]. Step 3: Process vertex 1: color=1, neighbors [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: color=0 (different, OK). For neighbor 2: uncolored, set colors[2]=0, queue=[3,2]. Step 4: Process vertex 3: color=1, neighbors [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: color=0 (different, OK). Check neighbor 2: color=0 (different, OK). Step 5: Process vertex 2: color=0, neighbors [1,3]. Check neighbor 1: color=1 (different, OK). Check neighbor 3: color=1 (different, OK). Queue empty, all vertices colored without conflicts. Return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Color Assignment for a Connected Component
ladders.desc: BFS traversal that colors all vertices in a connected component starting from a source vertex.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs BFS coloring on a single connected component of the graph. Given a starting vertex, it assigns colors to all vertices reachable from it, ensuring no adjacent vertices share the same color. The intuition is that BFS explores vertices layer by layer, allowing us to color each layer alternately: if the current vertex has color 0, its neighbors get color 1, and vice versa. This works because in BFS, all vertices at the same distance from the source are processed together, naturally creating a bipartite coloring if possible. The algorithm uses a queue to manage BFS: start by coloring the source vertex with 0, enqueue it, then repeatedly dequeue a vertex, check all its neighbors, and for each uncolored neighbor, assign the opposite color and enqueue it. If a neighbor is already colored with the same color as the current vertex, we immediately detect a conflict and return false. Time complexity for this component is O(V_c + E_c) where V_c and E_c are vertices and edges in the component. Space complexity is O(V_c) for the queue and color tracking. This feeds into the full problem by being called for each connected component. Common mistakes include not using a proper queue (e.g., using list inefficiently), forgetting to mark vertices as visited via coloring, and incorrect color toggling logic. The real code pattern involves a while loop with queue operations and neighbor iteration.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1]. Set colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process vertex 0: color=0, neighbors [1,2]. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set colors[1]=1, queue=[1]. For neighbor 2: uncolored, set colors[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Process vertex 1: color=1, neighbors [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: color=0 (different, OK). Check neighbor 2: color=1 (same as vertex 1). Conflict found, return false.

Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1]. Set colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process vertex 0: color=0, neighbors [1]. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set colors[1]=1, queue=[1]. Step 3: Process vertex 1: color=1, neighbors [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: color=0 (different, OK). For neighbor 2: uncolored, set colors[2]=0, queue=[2]. Step 4: Process vertex 2: color=0, neighbors [1]. Check neighbor 1: color=1 (different, OK). Queue empty, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Color Conflict Detection During Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: The mechanism to check if adjacent vertices have the same color while traversing edges.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the heart of bipartite detection: during graph traversal, for each edge (u,v), we check if colors[u] == colors[v]. If yes, the graph is not bipartite because adjacent vertices share the same color, violating the 2-coloring requirement. The intuition is that a bipartite graph is equivalent to a 2-colorable graph, meaning we can assign two colors (e.g., 0 and 1) such that no edge connects vertices of the same color. During BFS or DFS, as we traverse edges, we compare colors of the current vertex and its neighbor. If the neighbor is uncolored, we assign the opposite color; if already colored, we check for equality. A conflict occurs when colors match, indicating an odd cycle or other structure preventing bipartiteness. This check must be performed for every edge exactly once. Time complexity is O(E) for the check itself, but integrated into traversal it becomes O(V+E). Space complexity is O(1) per check. This feeds into the BFS sub-routine by being the condition that triggers early termination. Common mistakes include checking colors only in one direction (need for undirected graphs), not handling uncolored neighbors properly, and incorrect comparison logic. The real code pattern is a simple if-statement inside neighbor iteration.

examples.input: Current vertex u=1 with color=1, neighbor v=2 with color=1, edge exists
examples.trace: Step: Check if colors[u] == colors[v]. Here, colors[1]=1 and colors[2]=1. Since 1 == 1, a conflict is detected. This means vertices 1 and 2 are adjacent but have the same color, so the graph is not bipartite. Return false immediately.

examples.input: Current vertex u=0 with color=0, neighbor v=1 with color=1, edge exists
examples.trace: Step: Check if colors[u] == colors[v]. Here, colors[0]=0 and colors[1]=1. Since 0 != 1, no conflict. Continue traversal. If neighbor was uncolored, we would assign color 1 to it.

testCases.input: u=0 color=0, v=1 color=1, edge exists
u=0 color=0, v=1 color=0, edge exists
u=2 color=1, v=3 color=1, edge exists
u=2 color=1, v=3 color=0, edge exists
u=0 color=0, v=2 color=1, edge exists
u=1 color=1, v=1 color=1, edge exists (self-loop)
u=4 color=0, v=5 color=0, edge exists
u=4 color=0, v=5 color=1, edge exists
u=0 color=0, v=1 color=-1 (uncolored), edge exists
u=3 color=1, v=4 color=-1 (uncolored), edge exists

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal with Queue for Graph
ladders.desc: Implement breadth-first search on a graph using a queue to visit vertices level by level.
ladders.explanation: BFS traversal is a fundamental graph algorithm that visits all vertices reachable from a starting vertex in level order: first the start vertex, then all its neighbors, then their neighbors, etc. The intuition is to use a queue (FIFO data structure) to manage the order of visitation. We start by enqueuing the source vertex and marking it as visited. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a vertex, process it (e.g., check its color), and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This ensures vertices are processed in increasing distance from the source. For bipartite detection, we integrate coloring into this process. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex is enqueued/dequeued once and each edge is examined once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and visited set. This feeds into the color assignment sub-routine by providing the traversal framework. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (causing infinite loops), using incorrect data structures for the queue, and not handling disconnected graphs. The real code pattern uses collections.deque in Python for efficient queue operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue 0, visited={0}, queue=[0]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, process neighbors [1,2]. Enqueue 1 (unvisited), visited={0,1}, queue=[1]. Enqueue 2 (unvisited), visited={0,1,2}, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, process neighbors [0,3]. 0 already visited, enqueue 3 (unvisited), visited={0,1,2,3}, queue=[2,3]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, process neighbors [0,3]. Both visited, no enqueue. Step 5: Dequeue 3, process neighbors [1,2]. Both visited. Queue empty, BFS complete.

Step 1: Enqueue 0, visited={0}, queue=[0]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, process neighbors [1]. Enqueue 1 (unvisited), visited={0,1}, queue=[1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, process neighbors [0,2]. 0 visited, enqueue 2 (unvisited), visited={0,1,2}, queue=[2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, process neighbors [1]. 1 visited. Queue empty, BFS complete.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a graph using adjacency list and iterate over neighbors of a vertex.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a common way to represent a graph where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices (neighbors). For an undirected graph with V vertices, we typically use a list of lists: graph[v] contains all vertices u such that edge (v,u) exists. The intuition is that this representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E) space) and allows quick access to all neighbors of a vertex. To iterate over neighbors of vertex v, we simply loop through graph[v]. This operation is fundamental for graph traversal algorithms like BFS. In bipartite detection, we need to check all edges, which involves iterating over each vertex's adjacency list. Time complexity to iterate all neighbors of a single vertex is O(degree(v)), and total for all vertices is O(E). Space complexity for the adjacency list is O(V+E). This feeds into BFS traversal by providing the graph structure. Common mistakes include using incorrect indexing (0-based vs 1-based), not handling undirected edges properly (adding both directions), and inefficient representations for dense graphs. The real code pattern in Python is a list of lists, e.g., graph = [[] for _ in range(V)].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]] for 3 vertices. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to graph[0] -> [[1],[],[]]; append 0 to graph[1] -> [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): append 2 to graph[1] -> [[1],[0,2],[]]; append 1 to graph[2] -> [[1],[0,2],[1]]. Step 4: Add edge (0,2): append 2 to graph[0] -> [[1,2],[0,2],[1]]; append 0 to graph[2] -> [[1,2],[0,2],[1,0]]. Final: [[1,2],[0,2],[0,1]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Basics: Vertices, Edges, and Adjacency
ladders.desc: Understand fundamental graph concepts: vertices (nodes), edges (connections), and adjacency relationship.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a mathematical structure consisting of vertices (also called nodes) and edges that connect pairs of vertices. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction, meaning if vertex A is connected to vertex B, then B is also connected to A. The adjacency relationship describes which vertices are directly connected by an edge: two vertices are adjacent if there is an edge between them. For example, in a social network graph, vertices represent people, and edges represent friendships (undirected). The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and bipartite graphs specifically model relationships between two distinct groups (like jobs and applicants). Key terms: V = number of vertices, E = number of edges. A vertex's degree is the number of edges incident to it. This foundation is essential because bipartite detection relies on checking adjacency between vertices. Time and space are not analyzed here as this is conceptual. This feeds into graph representation by defining what we need to represent. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected graphs, misunderstanding adjacency, and incorrect counting of vertices/edges. The real code pattern involves defining V and edges as input.

examples.output: Adjacency: 0 adjacent to 1; 1 adjacent to 0 and 2; 2 adjacent to 1
examples.trace: Step 1: List vertices: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1): vertices 0 and 1 are adjacent. Step 3: Edge (1,2): vertices 1 and 2 are adjacent. Step 4: Since undirected, adjacency is symmetric: from step 2, 1 is adjacent to 0; from step 3, 2 is adjacent to 1. No edge between 0 and 2, so they are not adjacent.

examples.output: Adjacency: A adjacent to B and C; B adjacent to A and C; C adjacent to B and A
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: A,B,C. Step 2: Edge (A,B): A and B adjacent. Step 3: Edge (B,C): B and C adjacent. Step 4: Edge (C,A): C and A adjacent. Step 5: By symmetry: from step 2, B adjacent to A; from step 3, C adjacent to B; from step 4, A adjacent to C. So each vertex is adjacent to both others.

testCases.expected: 0 adjacent to 1; 1 adjacent to 0 and 2; 2 adjacent to 1
0 adjacent to 1 and 2; 1 adjacent to 0; 2 adjacent to 0
0 adjacent to 1; 1 adjacent to 0 and 2; 2 adjacent to 1 and 3; 3 adjacent to 2
A adjacent to B and C; B adjacent to A and C; C adjacent to B and A
X adjacent to Y; Y adjacent to X; Z adjacent to none
1 adjacent to 2 and 4; 2 adjacent to 1 and 3; 3 adjacent to 2 and 4; 4 adjacent to 3 and 1
a adjacent to b, c, d; b adjacent to a; c adjacent to a; d adjacent to a
0 adjacent to 1; 1 adjacent to 0 and 2; 2 adjacent to 1 and 3; 3 adjacent to 2 and 4; 4 adjacent to 3

approaches.name: Union-Find with Bipartite Check O(V+E α(V))
approaches.summary: Utilize a disjoint-set data structure to group vertices into two sets based on edges, checking for cycles that violate bipartiteness. This approach leverages union-find operations for efficient set management.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bipartite Graph Check with Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using union-find to determine if a graph is bipartite by grouping vertices into two sets and checking for conflicts.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind using union-find for bipartite checking is that we can represent the two required color sets (say 0 and 1) as disjoint sets. For each edge (u, v), we need to ensure u and v are in opposite sets. We achieve this by maintaining that if u is in set 0, then v must be in set 1, and vice versa. We use union-find to group vertices that must be in the same set and to detect contradictions. The algorithm initializes a parent array for V vertices and a rank array for union by rank. For each edge (u, v), we find the roots of u and v. If they are the same, it means u and v are already forced to be in the same set, which violates bipartiteness because an edge requires them to be in opposite sets—so we return false. Otherwise, we union u with the 'opposite' of v (i.e., v + V to represent the opposite set) and v with the opposite of u. This ensures that u and v are placed in different sets. The time complexity is O(V + E α(V)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function, due to union-find operations on V vertices and E edges. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This approach is efficient and leverages the disjoint-set data structure to avoid explicit coloring. Common mistakes include not handling the opposite set representation correctly (e.g., using indices beyond V) or forgetting to initialize parent arrays. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the union-find operations and bipartite logic into a complete algorithm.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5] for V=3 (indices 0-2 for set 0, 3-5 for set 1), rank=[0,0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots different, union(0,1+3=4) and union(1,0+3=3). After union: parent=[0,0,2,1,0,5], rank updated. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, roots different, union(1,2+3=5) and union(2,1+3=4). After union: parent=[0,0,0,1,0,0], rank updated. Step 4: Edge [0,2]: find(0)=0, find(2)=0, roots same, conflict detected because edge requires opposite sets but they are in same set, return false.

Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7], rank all 0. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1+4=5), union(1,0+4=4). Parent becomes [0,0,2,3,1,0,6,7]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(1,2+4=6), union(2,1+4=5). Parent becomes [0,0,0,3,1,0,0,7]. Step 4: Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, union(2,3+4=7), union(3,2+4=6). Parent becomes [0,0,0,0,1,0,0,0]. Step 5: Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0, find(0)=0, roots same, but check: find(3) and find(0+4=4)=1, different, so no conflict. Continue, all edges processed, return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Operations with Bipartite Constraints
ladders.desc: Implement union and find functions adapted for bipartite checking, including handling opposite sets.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the union-find operations specifically tailored for bipartite graphs. The intuition is that we need to manage two sets per vertex: the original set (color 0) and its opposite set (color 1). We represent this by doubling the vertex count: for vertex i, i represents set 0, and i+V represents set 1. The find function uses path compression to efficiently locate the root of a set. The union function merges two sets using union by rank to keep the tree flat. For bipartite checking, when we process an edge (u, v), we union u with v+V (to put u in opposite set of v) and v with u+V (to put v in opposite set of u). This ensures that u and v are forced into different sets. The time complexity for each find is O(α(V)), and union is O(α(V)). Space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays of size 2V. Common mistakes include not initializing arrays correctly or mishandling indices for opposite sets. This feeds into L0 by providing the core data structure operations needed for the full algorithm.

examples.input: V=3, operations: union(0,4), union(1,3), find(0), find(1)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5], rank=[0,0,0,0,0,0]. Step 1: union(0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=4, roots different, rank[0]=0, rank[4]=0, set parent[4]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,2,3,0,5]. Step 2: union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=1, rank[1]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,2,1,0,5]. Step 3: find(0): path compression, root=0. Step 4: find(1): root=1.

examples.input: V=2, operations: union(0,3), union(1,2), find(0), find(1)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank all 0. Step 1: union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,2,0]. Step 2: union(1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, set parent[2]=1, rank[1]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,1,0]. Step 3: find(0): root=0. Step 4: find(1): root=1.

testCases.input: V=5, operations: union(0,6), union(6,7), union(7,8), find(8)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Bipartite Conflicts with Union-Find
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking if an edge causes a conflict by comparing roots in the union-find structure.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for bipartite checking with union-find is that a graph is bipartite if and only if it has no odd-length cycles. With union-find, we simulate coloring by grouping vertices into two sets. For each edge (u, v), we check if u and v are already in the same set. If find(u) equals find(v), it means a previous edge has forced them into the same set, but the current edge requires them to be in opposite sets (since an edge connects vertices that must have different colors in a bipartite graph). This contradiction indicates an odd cycle, so the graph is not bipartite. Otherwise, we union u with the opposite of v and v with the opposite of u to enforce different sets. The intuition is that we maintain consistency: if u is in set A, then v must be in set B, and vice versa. This logic is applied iteratively over all edges. Time complexity per edge is O(α(V)) for find operations. Space is O(V) for the union-find structure. Common mistakes include not checking the correct roots or misunderstanding the set representation. This feeds into L1 by being the decision-making part of the union-find operations.

examples.input: V=3, edges: [0,1] and [1,2] processed, check edge [0,2]
examples.trace: After processing [0,1] and [1,2]: sets: {0,2} in one group, {1} in opposite. For edge [0,2]: find(0)=root of set containing 0, find(2)=same root, so they are in same set. Since edge [0,2] requires them to be in opposite sets, conflict detected.

After processing first three edges: sets: {0,2} in one group, {1,3} in opposite. For edge [3,0]: find(3)=root of set containing 3, find(0)=root of set containing 0, roots are different, so no conflict. Union operations would enforce further.

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint-Set Data Structure (Union-Find)
ladders.desc: Implement the basic union-find data structure with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: The disjoint-set data structure, also known as union-find, is used to manage a collection of disjoint sets. It supports two main operations: find (to determine which set an element belongs to) and union (to merge two sets). The intuition is to represent each set as a tree, where each node points to its parent, and the root represents the set identifier. Path compression optimizes find by flattening the tree during searches, making future finds faster. Union by rank merges trees by attaching the shorter tree under the root of the taller tree to keep the tree balanced. This ensures near-constant time per operation, amortized O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function. The algorithm initializes parent[i] = i and rank[i] = 0 for all i. Find recursively finds the root and compresses the path. Union finds roots of two elements and merges them based on rank. Time complexity is O(α(n)) per operation, space is O(n). Common mistakes include not updating rank correctly or missing path compression. This feeds into L2 by providing the underlying data structure for managing sets in bipartite checking.

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2), union(1,3), find(3)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Step 1: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent becomes [0,0,2,3,4]. Step 2: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. Parent becomes [0,0,2,2,4]. Step 3: find(0): root=0. Step 4: find(2): root=2. Step 5: union(1,3): find(1)=0, find(3)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, set parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Parent becomes [0,0,0,2,4]. Step 6: find(3): path compression, root=0.

examples.input: n=4, operations: union(0,2), union(1,3), find(2), find(3)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank all 0. Step 1: union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, set parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,0,3]. Step 2: union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=1, rank[1]=1. Parent becomes [0,1,0,1]. Step 3: find(2): path compression, root=0. Step 4: find(3): root=1.

testCases.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), find(5)
n=7, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), union(4,5), union(0,3), find(5)
n=8, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(0,2), union(4,6), find(7)
n=9, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(0,2), union(4,6), union(0,4), find(8)
n=10, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(7,8), union(0,3), union(3,6), find(9)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation in union-find that returns the root of a set and compresses the path.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is fundamental to union-find. Given an element x, it returns the root of the set containing x. The intuition is that each set is represented as a tree, where each node points to its parent, and the root points to itself. To find the root, we recursively follow parent pointers until reaching the root. Path compression optimizes this by making all nodes on the path point directly to the root during the find, flattening the tree and speeding up future operations. This reduces the time complexity to amortized O(α(n)). The algorithm: if parent[x] != x, set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) (recursive compression), then return parent[x]. This ensures that after a find, all nodes on the path have their parent updated to the root. Time complexity is O(α(n)) due to compression, space is O(1) extra per call. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if not handling base case or not updating parent correctly. This feeds into L3 as a core component of the union-find data structure.

examples.trace: Step 1: find(4): parent[4]=3 != 4, so call find(3). Step 2: find(3): parent[3]=2 != 3, call find(2). Step 3: find(2): parent[2]=1 != 2, call find(1). Step 4: find(1): parent[1]=0 != 1, call find(0). Step 5: find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Step 6: Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, return 0. Step 7: Backtrack: set parent[2]=0, return 0. Step 8: Backtrack: set parent[3]=0, return 0. Step 9: Backtrack: set parent[4]=0, return 0. Final parent becomes [0,0,0,0,0].
Step 1: find(2): parent[2]=2, so return 2. No compression needed.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets and Partitions
ladders.desc: Understand the basic idea of partitioning elements into disjoint sets and representing them.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves grouping elements into sets such that each element belongs to exactly one set, and sets do not overlap (disjoint). The intuition is to categorize items based on some property or relationship. For example, in a graph, vertices can be partitioned into two sets for bipartite checking. We represent sets using identifiers, often as integers, where each element has a parent pointer to another element in the same set, eventually pointing to a root that represents the set. This tree-like structure allows efficient set operations. Basic operations include checking if two elements are in the same set (by comparing their roots) and merging two sets (by making one root point to the other). This is the precursor to union-find. Time complexity for naive implementations can be O(n), but optimizations lead to better performance. Space complexity is O(n) to store parent pointers. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists or not maintaining disjointness. This feeds into L4 by introducing the idea behind the find operation.

examples.output: sets represented as roots: 0 for {0,1}, 2 for {2,3,4}
examples.trace: Step 1: Assign parent pointers: for set {0,1}, let parent[0]=0 (root), parent[1]=0. For set {2,3,4}, let parent[2]=2 (root), parent[3]=2, parent[4]=2. Step 2: Check if 1 and 3 are in same set: find root of 1 is 0, root of 3 is 2, different, so different sets. Step 3: Merge sets: make root of {0,1} point to root of {2,3,4}: set parent[0]=2. Now all elements have root 2.

examples.output: sets: root a for {a,b}, root c for {c,d}
examples.trace: Step 1: Represent as indices: a=0,b=1,c=2,d=3. Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=0 for {a,b}. Set parent[2]=2, parent[3]=2 for {c,d}. Step 2: Check if b and d in same set: root of b is 0, root of d is 2, different. Step 3: Merge: set parent[0]=2. Now all have root 2.
